content:
# Revelation Chapter 13 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 286-287 | **Source Image:** REV_013_page_001.jpg, REV_013_page_002.jpg

1. And I stood on the sand of the sea. And I saw a beast rising out of the sea, having ten horns and seven heads, and upon its horns ten diadems, and upon its heads names of blasphemy.

2. And the beast that I saw was like a leopard, and its feet were like those of a bear, and its mouth like the mouth of a lion. And the dragon gave it his power and his throne and great authority.

3. And one of its heads appeared as though slain to death, and its wound of death was healed. And the whole earth marveled after the beast.

4. And they worshipped the dragon because he gave authority to the beast, and they worshipped the beast, saying, "Who is like the beast, and who is able to wage war against it?"

5. And there was given to it a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies, and it was given authority to act for forty-two months.

6. And it opened its mouth in blasphemies against God, to blaspheme his name and his tabernacle, those who dwell in heaven.

7. And it was given to it to make war with the saints and to overcome them, and there was given to it authority over every tribe and people and tongue and nation.

8. And all who dwell upon the earth will worship it, everyone whose name has not been written in the book of life of the Lamb who was slain from the foundation of the world.

9. If anyone has an ear, let him hear.

10. If anyone is destined for captivity, into captivity he goes. If anyone is to be killed with the sword, with the sword he must be killed. Here is the endurance and the faith of the saints.

11. And I saw another beast rising out of the earth, and it had two horns like a lamb, and it spoke like a dragon.

12. And it exercises all the authority of the first beast in its presence, and it makes the earth and those who dwell in it worship the first beast, whose wound of death was healed.

13. And it performs great signs, so that it even makes fire come down from heaven to the earth in the presence of men.

14. And it deceives those who dwell upon the earth through the signs which were given to it to perform in the presence of the beast, telling those who dwell upon the earth to make an image to the beast who had the wound of the sword and yet lived.

15. And it was given to it to give breath to the image of the beast, so that the image of the beast might even speak, and cause as many as would not worship the image of the beast to be killed.

16. And it causes all, the small and the great, the rich and the poor, the free and the slaves, to be given a mark on their right hand or on their forehead,

17. and that no one should be able to buy or sell except the one having the mark, the name of the beast, or the number of its name.

18. Here is wisdom. Let the one who has understanding calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and its number is six hundred sixty-six.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**13:9** -- All three translators agreed on the substance of this verse. EMET and LOGOS rendered it almost identically ("If anyone has an ear, let him hear"); PNEUMA paraphrased ("Whoever has ears must listen carefully"). The consensus follows the more traditional phrasing shared by EMET and LOGOS, which closely mirrors the Sinaiticus Greek.

**13:4** -- All three translators agreed on the core content: worship of the dragon and the beast, and the dual rhetorical question. Differences were purely stylistic (see Disagreement Log).

**13:17** -- All three agreed on the essential meaning: the mark is required for commerce and consists of either the beast's name or the number of its name. Minor phrasing differences were resolved in favor of a close formal rendering.

---
## Disagreement Log

**13:1 -- "I stood" and opening phrasing:**
- EMET: "And I stood upon the sand of the sea. And I saw a beast coming up out of the sea..."
- LOGOS: "And I saw a beast rising out of the sea..." (omits the "I stood" clause)
- PNEUMA: "Then I took my stand on the sand at the edge of the sea. And I watched as a beast came rising up out of the ocean..."
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA in retaining the "I stood" clause, as Sinaiticus reads estathen (first person). LOGOS omitted it without note. The verb "rising" (LOGOS) is preferred over "coming up" (EMET) for English fluency while retaining the Sinaiticus reading. "Sea" is retained over PNEUMA's "ocean."

**13:1 -- "names of blasphemy" distribution:**
- EMET: "upon the heads of it names of blasphemy"
- LOGOS: "upon his heads names of blasphemy"
- PNEUMA: "on every head a blasphemous title"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in preserving the plural "names of blasphemy" directly, as this matches Sinaiticus onomata. PNEUMA's singular "title" distributed over "every head" conveys the same idea dynamically but departs from the form.

**13:2 -- Pronoun for the beast:**
- EMET: "it" throughout, with "the feet of it," "the mouth of it"
- LOGOS: "his" throughout -- "his feet," "his mouth"
- PNEUMA: "its feet were massive like a bear's and its mouth gaped wide"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "its" for the beast, following PNEUMA's neuter pronoun which is contextually appropriate for a beast. LOGOS's masculine "his" reflects Greek grammatical gender but over-personalizes in English. EMET's construction is too stilted.

**13:3 -- Description of the healed wound:**
- EMET: "one of the heads of it as having been slain to death, and the wound of the death of it was healed"
- LOGOS: "one of his heads was as though slain unto death, and his wound of death was healed"
- PNEUMA: "One of the beast's heads looked as though it had been dealt a fatal blow, yet the death-wound had healed"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "appeared as though slain to death" -- blending EMET's participial "having been slain" with LOGOS's "as though slain" for clarity, and retaining "wound of death" from EMET/LOGOS rather than PNEUMA's "death-wound" compound.

**13:4 -- "wage war" vs. "make war":**
- EMET: "make war with it"
- LOGOS: "wage war with him"
- PNEUMA: "wage war against it"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "wage war against it" -- "wage war" is preferred over "make war" for English idiom (with LOGOS and PNEUMA); "against" is clearer than "with" for the adversarial sense; "it" for the beast (with EMET and PNEUMA) rather than "him."

**13:5 -- "to act" vs. "to exercise its power":**
- EMET: "authority to act for forty-two months"
- LOGOS: "authority to act for forty-two months"
- PNEUMA: "permitted to exercise its power for forty-two months"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS with "authority to act," which is closer to the Greek poiesai. PNEUMA's expansion to "exercise its power" adds interpretive content.

**13:6 -- Blasphemy clause structure:**
- EMET: "to blaspheme the name of him and the tabernacle of him, the ones tabernacling in the heaven"
- LOGOS: "to blaspheme his name and his tabernacle, those who dwell in heaven"
- PNEUMA: "slandering his name, his dwelling place, and all those who have their home in heaven"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's phrasing, which preserves the appositional structure (tabernacle = those dwelling in heaven) while being readable. PNEUMA's "slandering" and "dwelling place" depart from the Greek vocabulary. EMET's literal "tabernacling" is preserved in the notes.

**13:7 -- "holy ones" vs. "saints" vs. "God's holy people":**
- EMET: "the holy ones"
- LOGOS: "the saints"
- PNEUMA: "God's holy people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "the saints," following LOGOS, as the standard English rendering of hoi hagioi in biblical translation. This recurs throughout the chapter (v. 10).

**13:7 -- "conquer" vs. "overcome" vs. "overpower":**
- EMET: "to conquer them"
- LOGOS: "to overcome them"
- PNEUMA: "to overpower them"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "to overcome them" (LOGOS), which balances EMET's more martial "conquer" and PNEUMA's physical "overpower."

**13:8 -- "scroll of life" vs. "book of life" vs. "Book of Life":**
- EMET: "the scroll of life of the Lamb"
- LOGOS: "the book of life of the Lamb"
- PNEUMA: "the Book of Life belonging to the Lamb"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "the book of life of the Lamb" (LOGOS). While "scroll" (EMET) is technically more accurate for biblion, "book of life" is the established English phrase.

**13:8 -- "slain" vs. "slaughtered":**
- EMET: "having been slain"
- LOGOS: "who has been slain"
- PNEUMA: "who was slaughtered"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "who was slain" -- combining the relative clause form (LOGOS/PNEUMA) with "slain" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's more visceral "slaughtered."

**13:9 -- Phrasing of the hearing call:**
- EMET: "If anyone has an ear, let him hear."
- LOGOS: "If anyone has an ear, let him hear."
- PNEUMA: "Whoever has ears must listen carefully:"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS exactly. PNEUMA's paraphrase ("must listen carefully") adds interpretive force not in the Greek.

**13:10 -- Captivity saying:**
- EMET: "If anyone leads into captivity, into captivity he goes. If anyone is to be killed with the sword, with the sword he must be killed."
- LOGOS: "If anyone is destined for captivity, into captivity he goes. If anyone is to be killed with the sword, with the sword he must be killed."
- PNEUMA: "If anyone is bound for captivity, into captivity they will go. If anyone is to be killed by the sword, by the sword they will die."
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's rendering. "Destined for" captures the compressed Sinaiticus text better than EMET's literal "[leads] into." PNEUMA's plural "they" shifts from the Greek singular.

**13:10 -- "endurance and faith of the saints":**
- EMET: "the endurance and the faith of the holy ones"
- LOGOS: "the endurance and the faith of the saints"
- PNEUMA: "the call for God's people to stand firm in endurance and faith"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's expansion to "the call for God's people to stand firm" is interpretive paraphrase.

**13:11 -- "coming up" vs. "rising" vs. "climbing up":**
- EMET: "coming up out of the earth"
- LOGOS: "rising out of the earth"
- PNEUMA: "climbing up out of the earth"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "rising out of the earth" (LOGOS) for consistency with v. 1 and for dignity of style.

**13:11 -- "was speaking as a dragon" vs. "spoke like a dragon":**
- EMET: "it was speaking as a dragon"
- LOGOS: "he spoke like a dragon"
- PNEUMA: "when it spoke, it sounded like a dragon"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "it spoke like a dragon" -- combining the neuter "it" with LOGOS's concise phrasing.

**13:12 -- "before it" vs. "in his presence" vs. "on its behalf":**
- EMET: "before it"
- LOGOS: "in his presence"
- PNEUMA: "on its behalf"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "in its presence," following LOGOS's phrasing with the neuter pronoun. The Greek enopion means "before/in the presence of," not "on behalf of" (PNEUMA's interpretive choice).

**13:13 -- "fire come down" and audience:**
- EMET: "fire it makes to come down out of the heaven to the earth before the people"
- LOGOS: "he even makes fire come down from heaven to the earth in the presence of men"
- PNEUMA: "even making fire fall from the sky to the earth while everyone watched"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's phrasing. "Heaven" is retained over PNEUMA's "sky." "In the presence of men" preserves the Greek enopion ton anthropon.

**13:14 -- "telling" vs. "saying" vs. "persuading":**
- EMET: "saying to the ones dwelling upon the earth to make an image"
- LOGOS: "telling those who dwell upon the earth to make an image"
- PNEUMA: "persuading them to construct an image in honor of the beast"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "telling." PNEUMA's "persuading" and "construct" and "in honor of" add interpretive layers.

**13:14 -- "lived" vs. "came back to life":**
- EMET: "who has the wound of the sword and lived"
- LOGOS: "who has the wound of the sword and yet lived"
- PNEUMA: "that had survived the sword-wound and come back to life"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "yet lived," which adds a concessive nuance faithful to the Greek. PNEUMA's "come back to life" is interpretive.

**13:15 -- "spirit" vs. "breath" vs. "life":**
- EMET: "to give spirit to the image"
- LOGOS: "to give breath to the image"
- PNEUMA: "to breathe life into the image"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "breath" (LOGOS). The Greek pneuma can mean spirit or breath; "breath" fits the context of animating an image. PNEUMA's "breathe life into" is a dynamic expansion.

**13:16 -- "mark" vs. "brand":**
- EMET: "a mark upon the right hand of them or upon the forehead of them"
- LOGOS: "a mark on their right hand or on their forehead"
- PNEUMA: "a brand on their right hand or on their forehead"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "a mark" (EMET/LOGOS). The Greek charagma is traditionally rendered "mark." PNEUMA's "brand" is interpretive.

**13:16 -- "to be given a mark" vs. "to receive a brand":**
- EMET: "that they should give to them a mark"
- LOGOS: "to be given a mark"
- PNEUMA: "to receive a brand"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's passive construction "to be given a mark," which smoothly captures the Greek didosin autois.

**13:18 -- Phrasing of the wisdom call:**
- EMET: "The one having understanding, let him count the number"
- LOGOS: "Let him who has understanding calculate the number"
- PNEUMA: "Let the person who has understanding calculate the beast's number"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's phrasing. "Calculate" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is preferred over "count" (EMET) for psephisato. PNEUMA's "the person" is unnecessarily expanded.

**13:18 -- "six hundred sixty-six" vs. "666":**
- EMET: "six hundred sixty-six"
- LOGOS: "six hundred sixty-six"
- PNEUMA: "666"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA writes the number out as "six hundred sixty-six" (EMET/LOGOS), preserving the written-out Greek form hexakosioi hexekonta hex.

---

-- SOPHIA
